misconceptionSummary: Students add the perimeters of the component rectangles and do not subtract the shared internal edges.

visuals.ruleText: When two shapes are joined, the shared edge disappears from both boundaries — subtract it twice
visuals.subText: Each shape loses one edge at the join — total perimeter = P₁ + P₂ − 2 × shared edge

steps.narration: When two rectangles are placed side by side, the edge where they touch is no longer on the boundary of either shape. It disappears from both perimeters — so it must be subtracted twice from the combined total.

lines.text: Two 6×4 rectangles joined along a 4 cm edge

steps.narration: Each rectangle has perimeter twenty. Together they would be forty — but the four-centimetre join is hidden on both sides. Subtract twice four: forty minus eight is thirty-two centimetres.
Tracing the outer boundary of the compound shape and adding each edge directly confirms the thirty-two centimetre answer. Both methods agree — the formula is reliable.

visuals.label: Joining removes the shared edge from both boundaries

steps.narration: Joining two shapes removes the shared edge from both perimeters. Subtract it twice from the sum of the individual perimeters.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: P = P₁ + P₂ without subtracting the shared join. The join edge disappears from both shapes — subtract it twice: P = P₁ + P₂ − 2 × shared.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: When shapes join, the shared edge vanishes from the boundary of each shape. Subtract it twice — once for each shape that loses it.
